description: Behavior cases for single server-mediated write-path (command bus) design: fixed pipeline order, server-derived actor + tenant scope, idempotency vs concurrency, the no-direct-client-writes invariant, and the exactly-once refusal.

cases.prompt: Our mutations are spread across dozens of endpoints and a client SDK that writes to tables directly. Each one re-implements its own checks and they've drifted. Design a single write path so every protected mutation is validated, authorized, and audited consistently.

assertions: Inventories COMMANDS (protected mutations) rather than endpoints, and defines one per-command pipeline with a fixed order: validate → authenticate actor from token → authorize → derive tenant/resource scope from trusted rows → idempotency → execute → emit audit + events → safe error envelope.
States the no-direct-client-writes invariant AND names the datastore backstop (RLS/write-deny) that makes the gateway the sole write authority, not merely the polite one.
Delegates the authorization policy to authorization-matrix-designer and the audit record schema to audit-log-architect rather than re-authoring them.
Produces an enforcement-point map (command × stage) and flags any write that reads scope from the body or skips audit as a finding.

cases.prompt: On our update-record endpoint the client sends the tenant_id and owner_id in the JSON body and we use them to scope the write. Fix the write path.

assertions: Identifies client-supplied tenant_id/owner_id on a write as the cross-tenant / privilege-escalation bug and removes the trust: scope is derived by looking the target row up and reading its tenant_id server-side.
Orders derive-scope-then-authorize so authorization runs against the REAL row's tenant, not a client-claimed target.
Returns a uniform not-found/forbidden envelope that does not reveal whether the row exists in another tenant (no existence oracle).
Does not merely add a validation check on the body value — the body value is untrusted regardless of shape.

cases.prompt: Retries create duplicate charges, and separately two users editing the same record clobber each other. Design the write path to handle both.

assertions: Treats retries with an idempotency contract (stable key source, dedup store with retention, replayed key returns the first result without re-executing side effects) — NOT keyed on the whole request body.
Treats concurrent DIFFERENT writers as a separate problem needing optimistic version check or row lock, and states explicitly that idempotency is not concurrency control.
Names both mechanisms per affected command rather than conflating them.
Ensures the side-effecting execute and its audit/event emission are transactional so a replay or a crash cannot half-apply.

cases.prompt: Make every command exactly-once end to end — including the confirmation emails and the third-party payment call. Just guarantee it in the gateway.

assertions: Refuses an unqualified end-to-end exactly-once guarantee across external side effects (emails, third-party charges) — it is not purchasable at the gateway.
Offers the honest decomposition: at-least-once execution with idempotent effects at each edge, and where duplicate suppression actually lives for the payment and email cases.
Escalates the surviving hard requirement to a human decision (Stop Condition) rather than writing a false guarantee into the design.

cases.prompt: Design our public REST API: resource naming, versioning and deprecation policy, per-plan rate limits, and the webhook envelope partners subscribe to.

assertions: Does not invoke command-gateway-architect: resource naming, versioning, rate limits, and partner webhooks are the EXTERNAL contract owned by api-event-architect.
The gateway is what those endpoints dispatch into, but nothing here asks for the internal write-path pipeline.
